title: COVID-19 origin clues buried in ludicrous lobster theory
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Tiny but mighty magnets, ravens don’t follow wolves, and more
Brought to you by Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
View this email in your browser
16 March 2026
Today’s Visualized explores the relationship between ravens and wolves in Yellowstone National Park. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including some flaky physics and how a ludicrous claim about lobsters has helped reveal new information about the market where COVID-19 got its start.
physics  |  physics of fluids
The hidden physics of rooftop snowdrifts
When snow gathers, it rarely settles evenly. Wind sweeps some areas bare, while drifts pile high in others. If on top of a roof or structure, these patterns can determine whether a structure safely holds the weight or risks collapse. According to new research, the size of the snowflakes themselves may shape how the mass accumulates.

Engineers typically model snowfall as if every snow particle were the same size. But in reality, snowstorms produce a mix of flakes that vary widely in diameter. To see how that variability affects accumulation, researchers developed a numerical model and simulated how particles of different sizes move through turbulent air and settle on rooftops. They then tested the model’s findings in a wind tunnel using silica particles that mimic snow. By releasing particles of different sizes and exposing them to varying wind speeds, the team observed how snowlike material gathered and redistributed across model roofs.

The results showed that larger particles tend to accumulate more easily, especially under strong winds. Smaller particles are more likely to be carried away, while larger ones resist the airflow and settle more readily. Wind also reshapes the deposits after they land, redistributing snow across the roof surface. Larger roofs can trap more particles and build deeper snow layers, particularly when particle diameters are around half a millimeter. The findings could help improve building codes and structural design in snowy regions, where accurately estimating snow loads is critical for safety.
Read the Paper
covid-19  |  Scienceinsider
COVID-19 origin clues buried in ludicrous lobster theory
A food stall owner at a night market in Wuhan in December 2019, which was soon to become the epicenter of the COVID-19 outbreak.  qilai shen/panos pictures/redux
When an anonymous post on a Chinese social media website in September 2021 contended that frozen lobsters from Maine sparked COVID-19, it promptly was dismissed as a laughable attempt to distance China from the pandemic’s origin. Evolutionary biologist Florence Débarre, who works at the French National Research Agency known as CNRS and has extensively studied the origin question, saw the post as “extremely elaborate disinformation.” But delving deeply into it recently, Débarre has uncovered that the message on WeChat from someone calling themselves Kunlun Sword (a martial arts reference) may have had inside information about the earliest known infected animals and people that China still has never shared—and could help clarify how SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19, jumped into humans.

An analysis of the WeChat post that Débarre has shared with some colleagues—and today posted online—examines maps of SARS-CoV-2’s spread at the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market in Wuhan, China, which became world famous after Chinese authorities reported it was linked to a cluster of cases of a mysterious respiratory infection on 31 December 2019. Debates have raged over whether virus-infected animals at the food market or a leak from a nearby lab sparked the COVID-19 pandemic. Débarre has now translated and carefully checked the detailed annotations on the market maps, showing that the WeChat post had information that was only made public over the next few years. More important, the maps identify specific stalls as having live animals infected with SARS-CoV-2 and vendors with antibodies to the virus—data China has never shared.

Although Débarre thinks her finding adds to what she sees as abundant evidence that infected animals at the market sparked the pandemic, she realizes the new information will not end the debate; infected humans, after all, could have spread the virus to the animals for sale at the market. Still, the origins impasse has suffered mightily from a dearth of new data, and the analysis ultimately suggests China may be able to fill in some missing puzzle pieces about the pandemic’s start.
Read the Full Story
engineering  |  Science advances
Magnificent mini magnets
Science and medicine require some pretty big magnets. The magnets in the largest experimental MRI machines can surpass 4000 kilograms, and the Large Hadron Collider particle accelerator has more than 1500 magnets spanning five to 15 meters each. Now, scientists have effectively shrink-rayed all that strength into a superstrong magnet the size of your palm.

To make the magnet, researchers wound a tape coated with a superconducting material called rare-earth barium copper oxide (REBCO) around a cylinder the width of a pencil. They used a special winding technique that created pancake-shaped coils from the REBCO, eliminating any sharp joints where strength could be lost or the superconducting layer could crack. A two-coil mini magnet demonstrated a strength of 38 tesla, and a four-coil version had a strength of 42 tesla (a typical refrigerator magnet’s strength is about 0.005 Tesla). The new magnets also required less than 1 watt of power, while superstrong magnets typically require megawatts.

“Producing magnetic fields above 40 Tesla traditionally requires very large and expensive facilities, so achieving similar field strengths in such a compact device using superconducting tapes is significant,” engineer Mark Ainslie, who was not involved with the work, told New Scientist. “It suggests that extremely high-field magnets could become more accessible to a wider range of laboratories in the near future.”
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology: Call for Entries 2026
This prize is awarded to young scientists for their outstanding contributions to neurobiological research based on experimental methods of molecular, cellular, systems, or organismic biology. Researchers not older than 35 years are invited to apply.
Apply Now
Visualized
Ravens and wolves have shared Yellowstone’s landscape since wolves were reintroduced in the mid-1990s.  jim peaco
In Norse mythology, the god Odin sent two ravens named Huginn and Muninn—Thought and Memory—across the world each morning to gather intelligence while accompanied by his wolves. And according to new research published in Science, there may be some truth to this ancient tale: Rather than shadowing wolf packs to fresh kills, ravens rely on spatial memory to navigate toward landscapes where wolves are most likely to hunt.

The study overturns a widely held assumption about how scavengers find food. Ravens are a constant presence at Yellowstone wolf kills, sometimes arriving before the pack has even finished feeding. Rangers, naturalists, and wildlife documentarians have long concluded that the birds must be following the wolves. But nobody had ever tracked both species simultaneously across a landscape for long enough to know.
Ravens are frequently seen near traveling wolf packs, but the new study suggests this reflects short-range opportunism rather than a sustained foraging strategy.  daniel stahler
To find out, researchers GPS-tagged 69 ravens, 20 wolves, and 11 cougars in Yellowstone over 2.5 years. Ravens are acutely observant of their surroundings and deeply suspicious of anything out of place, so researchers had to match each trap to its environment and bait them with trash and fast food. The effort yielded more than 646,000 raven location fixes alongside records of 355 wolf kills and 137 cougar kills.

Across the entire study, they recorded exactly one instance of long-distance following. Ravens, it turns out, were not shadowing the wolves. Rather than tailing predators in real time, ravens repeatedly returned from up to 155 kilometers away to areas with historically high wolf kill density, navigating with the same directional precision they use to find landfills and other permanent food sources. Individual ravens returned to the same wolf territories repeatedly, sometimes after gaps of nearly a year. The preference was also specific to predator: 48.5% of wolf kills were visited by at least one tagged raven within seven days, compared to 24.8% of cougar kills, a gap the authors attribute to wolves’ visibility as pack hunters in open terrain versus cougars’ solitary, forest-dwelling habits.
A raven being fitted with a solar-powered GPS backpack, weighing just 35 grams. Tracking 69 individuals over 2.5 years yielded more than 646,000 location fixes.  john marzluff
“This changes how we think about how scavengers find food and suggests we may have underestimated some species for a long time,” said wildlife biologist John Marzluff, who served as the senior author of the new study in a statement.

Lead author Matthias-Claudio Loretto explained on Science’s weekly podcast that, while the raven location data is publicly available, information on the predators has to be treated with more care. “We cannot share that data because it might facilitate hunters finding the right location of the animals,” he told podcast host Sarah Crespi.
listen to the related Science podcast
Read the Paper
Et Cetera
Hazard map scrapped
The Indian government has withdrawn a new earthquake hazard map and related building codes just a few months after issuing them, sparking concerns from geophysicists.
Read more at ScienceInsider
Ix-nay on the x-ray
NASA has canceled a $1 billion project to build the next x-ray space telescope, citing budget and scheduling problems.
Read more at ScienceInsider
Friends in high places
A genetic mutation that helps yaks and other animals cope with low oxygen levels at high altitudes could help scientists treat brain diseases like multiple sclerosis.
Neuron Paper  |  Read more at Science News
Imagine if you found the last person on Earth who spoke a language—you’d want to interview them … This ice is that person.
—Roger Creel, Texas A&M University
NEWS FEATURE  |  12 March 2026  |  evan howell
Ancient glacial ice melting out of Alaska’s eroding coastline could present scientists with a rare time capsule from a period of profound natural climatic upheaval.
Last but not least
When it comes to mass extinctions, you’d reckon something called the Great Dying would be the worst of the worst. But Earth’s first extinction event, known as the Kotlin Crisis, may have been far more extensive than previously thought—potentially rivaling its more recent, famous counterparts in terms of percentage of animals lost.
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, Science

With contributions from Ana Georgescu, Jon Cohen, and Hannah Richter

superstrong magnet the size of your palm
may have been far more extensive than previously thought
